coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of reduced-graphene oxide quantum dots and application for colorimetric biosensor
coredata.dc:creator: Hwang, Juil
Le, Anh Duy Duong
Trinh, Cong Tai
Le, Quynh Thuy
Lee, Kwang-Geol
Kim, Jaeyong
coredata.dc:description: We report a one-pot synthesis method to produce reduced-graphene oxide quantum dots (rGOQDs) in perfluorotributylamine (PFTBA) solution by avoiding the use of toxic materials and preventing the generation of undesirable functional groups (epoxy, carbonyl, and carboxyl). The PFTBA solution, consisting of three butyl fluoride groups connected to an amine center, effectively cracked down rGO precursors at ∼ 160 °C, and produced high purity of rGOQDs. Under UV irradiation, the location of the emission peak of the photoluminescence (PL) shifted from 489.3 nm to 460.8 nm as the solution temperature increased from 160 °C to 190 °C due to the enhancement of the bandgap. The produced sample exhibited a long-term photostability without the decay in intensity over 30 days, which can be used for an optical device. Depending on the oxidation state, PL spectra from the rGOQDs-solution mixed with glucose oxidase (GOx) changed the color from blue to green with high sensitivity (3.869 deg/mM) in the glucose concentration of 0–2 millimole, demonstrating that rGOQDs can be applied for colorimetric biosensors using a portable smartphone.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
rGOQD
Green material
Glucose
Colorimetry

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst vitae

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for the synthesis of rGOQDs using rGOs as a precursor. Chemical configurations and fluorescence images under UV irradiation of for rGOs and rGOQDs are shown in (a) and (b), respectively. The rGOs were immersed into non-toxic perfluorotributylamine (PFTBA) solvent and exposed to UV light to exfoliate them into thin ayers. Hydrothermal processing was used to crack down the precursor into small particles.

Geometric characteristics of rGOQDs determined by using TEM and AFM. (a) A typical TEM image of rGOQDs and a high-resolution image in the inset. (b) Histogram of the diameter of rGOQDs with a Gaussian fitting to find the lateral size of 3.4 ± 0.4 nm. (c) An AFM image of rGOQDs distributed on Si substrate. (d) Height profile of rGOQDs along the red line of the image in (c). The average height is 1.6 nm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Results of structural and compositional analyses of rGOs and rGOQDs. (a) XRD and (b) Raman spectra of precursor rGOs (black) and rGOQDs (blue), in perfluorotributylamine (PFTBA) solution. (c) XPS spectra of rGOQDs solution. (d) High-resolution spectrum of C1s peak in (c). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Absorption (black) and PL (orange, green, blue, and purple) spectra of four rGOQDs samples prepared at different perfluorotributylamine solution temperatures (160, 170, 180, and 190 °C). The excitation wavelength was 365 nm. (b) Plots of PL emission spectra of rGOQDs measured at different excitation wavelengths in the 290–380 nm range. The 3D excitation-wavelength dependent fluorescence map is illustrated in the inset. (c) Spectrally integrated PL relaxation in solution fitted using a bi-exponentially decay fitting curve. (d) Result of the change of the peak intensity over 80 min of continuous UV irradiation demonstrating superior photostability. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Schematic diagram of colorimetric detection of H2O2 and glucose under combination of GO and rGOQDs. RGB intensity was measured using a UV-free smartphone camera. (b) Plots of chromaticity [r,g,b] and hue values of rGOQDs solution for various glucose concentrations. (c) Relative sensitivity (SH ) of hue values for glucose, fructose, sucrose, and minerals (Zn2+, K+, Ca2+, and Fe+). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure text, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Performance of glucose colorimetric biosensors based on graphene quantum dots.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of reduced-graphene oxide quantum dots and application for colorimetric biosensor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Hanyang University, Seoul, 04763, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Hanyang University, Seoul, 04763, Republic of Korea.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Hanyang University, Seoul, 04763, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Hanyang University, Seoul, 04763, Republic of Korea

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report a one-pot synthesis method to produce reduced-graphene oxide quantum dots (rGOQDs) in perfluorotributylamine (PFTBA) solution by avoiding the use of toxic materials and preventing the generation of undesirable functional groups (epoxy, carbonyl, and carboxyl). The PFTBA solution, consisting of three butyl fluoride groups connected to an amine center, effectively cracked down rGO precursors at ∼ 160 °C, and produced high purity of rGOQDs. Under UV irradiation, the location of the emission peak of the photoluminescence (PL) shifted from 489.3 nm to 460.8 nm as the solution temperature increased from 160 °C to 190 °C due to the enhancement of the bandgap. The produced sample exhibited a long-term photostability without the decay in intensity over 30 days, which can be used for an optical device. Depending on the oxidation state, PL spectra from the rGOQDs-solution mixed with glucose oxidase (GOx) changed the color from blue to green with high sensitivity (3.869 deg/mM) in the glucose concentration of 0–2 millimole, demonstrating that rGOQDs can be applied for colorimetric biosensors using a portable smartphone.

ce:section.ce:para: Owing to their excellent morphological and electron transport properties of quantum dots (QDs), including zero-dimensional properties [1,2], quantum confinement, and edge effects [3], reduced graphene oxides (rGO) and their related reduced-graphene oxide quantum dots (rGOQDs) have recently been extensively studied as a new emerging material for optoelectronic applications [4–6]. Because of their functional properties of size-tunable photoluminescence (PL), chemical inertness, and good water solubility, rGOQDs are proposed as good candidates for multi-purpose sensing materials. To synthesize functional rGOQDs, large sheets of graphene, graphene oxides (GOs), or reduced graphene oxides (rGOs) should be cut into nanometer-scale pieces [7–10], and a hydrothermal method should be employed to treat the carbon precursors in harsh environments. However, most of these preparation processes are complex and environmentally deleterious, and involve oxidative treatment in the presence of nitric and/or sulfuric acids. To comply with the new trend in utilizing nanomaterials, the precursors are usually treated using the Hummer method [11–14]. During the process, however, the produced graphene quantum dots (GQDs) retain considerable amounts of impurities (Mn2+, Fe3+, and NH3), which significantly decrease the optical and electro-catalytic performance of rGOQDs. As such, it is desirable to design a simple synthesis protocol for producing GQDs, which excludes the use of toxic materials, such as acids, ethanol, ammonia, or potassium permanganate, and which reduces the generation of impurities.

Recent studies on the fabrication of GQDs have suggested that fluorine (F)-containing materials effectively crack down graphene [15]. Because F atoms possess larger binding and desorption energies for C than those of H atoms, using diethylaminosulfur trifluoride will reduce the amount of agents (carbonyl or carboxylic groups) during the synthesis [16]. In this study, we proposed a facile one-pot synthesis method for fabricating rGOQDs from rGOs using perfluorotributylamine (PFTBA, CT-Solv180) as a green solvent [17]. The samples were processed in the same solution throughout the synthesis process. This new synthesis protocol employed non-toxic materials only. Further, the size of the rGOQDs was systematically controlled by changing the temperature of the solution during the synthesis. The optical properties of rGOQDs prepared by the current method were investigated with the aim of developing a practical application for a QD-based colorimetric device that would be used as a glucose-detecting sensor.

After synthesizing rGOs by processing graphene oxide (GO) through the processes of ultra-sonication for 40 min with a hydrothermal treatment for six hours, approximately 1 mg of rGOs powder having an average size of ∼3.1 μm was grinded and dissolved in 3 mL of perfluorotributylamine solvent (PFTBA, CT-Solv180) purchased from AGC chemical. The precursor-containing solution was first subjected to sonication and UV light irradiation to initiate the cracking-down process. After the rGO powder was completely dispersed in the solvent through sonication, the solution was exposed to UV irradiation for 20 min to exfoliate the rGOs powder into thin layers in the organic solvent. Quantum dots were reduced to the size in order of a few angstroms by hydrothermal treatment at a temperature above 170 °C. After the solution was allowed to cool down to room temperature, the solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min to select out of large grains of rGOs. A schematic diagram illustrating the synthesis of rGOQDs is described in Fig. 1 .

The morphological characterization of the rGOQDs samples was analyzed by investigating images measured from a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL2100 F) and an atomic force microscope (AFM, XE200, Park system). The structural and compositional properties of the samples were determined by analyzing the data of X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Discovery), and the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo K-alpha plus). Optical properties were characterized by employing Raman spectroscopy (Renishaw Via the laser source of 532 nm). Excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence was measured by employing LS-55 (Perkin Elmer).

The rGOQDs particles were well-dispersed in the PFTBA solution due to the presence of F-containing effective crack-down material, and photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra data were measured under UV light irradiation. An analysis of a high-resolution TEM image (inset of Fig. 2 (a)) revealed that the interlayer d-spacing of the (100) hexagonal lattice of graphene planes is approximately 0.21 nm. The lateral size of the rGOQDs was 3.4 ± 0.4 nm with a Gaussian distribution (Fig. 2(b)), and the average height measured by AFM was 1.6 nm (Fig. 2(c) and (d)).

The XRD patterns of the rGOs and rGOQDs show a relatively broad peak locating at 2θ = 24.5°, which corresponds to the interlayer spacing of 0.38 nm, while one of rGOs (Fig. 3 (a)) presents interlayer spacing of 0.37 nm [18,19]. One needs to note that the suppression of a peak near 43 in two theta from rGOQDs suggests transforming the 2-dimensionality of rGO to nanoparticles. The average particle size of rGOQDs estimated using the Scherrer formula, T = 0.9λ/BcosθB , where, T, λ, B, and θB are the particle size in Å, X-ray wavelength of 1.542 Å, full width at half maximum (FWHM) value of the peak (= 6.34°), and diffraction angle in radian, respectively, was approximately 1.3 nm. The lattice vibrations of the sample measured by Raman spectroscopy are shown in Fig. 3(b). Before cracking-down, the rGO precursor exhibited two peaks at 1348.1 cm−1 and 1600.2 cm−1, corresponding to disordered (D-band) and ordered (G-band) graphite, respectively, while the peak positions of D- and G-bands in rGOQDs after cracking-down shifted slightly, to 1350.4 cm−1 and 1593.2 cm−1, respectively. It is due to the enhanced phonon frequency of breathing-mode caused by the reduced grain size after the hydrothermal reduction. Similar results were reported by T. V. Cuong et al. [20]. To evaluate the deformation of the graphite structure in rGOQDs, their relative peak intensities were compared because the G-band represents the in-plane vibration of the sp2 C atoms in graphitic materials, whereas the D-band represents the sp3 defects in the C–C bonds [21]. The G-band peak intensity (I G) of the rGOs precursor was higher than that of D-band (I D), with the I G /I D ratio being 1.15. This indicates that rGOs maintained the intrinsic properties of graphite structure and needed no further treatment as a precursor [22,23]. For the rGOQDs sample, the peak ratio, I G /I D, was even higher (1.46), and the values of FWHM for both the G- and D-bands were narrower compared with those of rGOs. The FWHM values of the D- and the G-bands of rGOQDs were 45 cm−1 and 41 cm−1, respectively, while those of rGOs were 87 cm−1 and 67 cm-1. The G-band of rGOQDs is attributed to the stretching vibrations from the in-plane sp2 carbon bonds in hybridized graphene, while the narrow D-band suggests the enhanced ordered structure in carbon-based materials [24,25]. The XPS spectrum of rGOQDs shown in Fig. 3(c) presents two distinct peaks at 286.9 eV and 532.3 eV, corresponding to C1s and O1s with atomic percentages of approximately 63.4 % and 34.4 %, respectively. It is known that functional groups of rGOQDs create defects during the synthesis [26]. The XPS spectrum measured from rGOQDs revealed that the C-1 s peak can be decomposed into three peaks locating at 284.2 eV, 285.9 eV, and 288.4 eV, which correspond to different functional groups of the aromatic ring of C=C/C–C, epoxy C–O, and carboxyl C=O/COOH, respectively (Fig. 3(d)) [27]. The much higher peak intensity of the C=C/C–C than those of the other bonding groups suggests that the formation of undesirable functional groups, such as carbonyl, carboxyl, and epoxy in rGOQDs was effectively suppressed. The XPS spectra for rGOs and the O1s for rGOQDs are included in the supplementary information (Figs. S1 and S2).

The mechanism of hydrothermal cutting of rGOs into rGOQDs associated with few functional groups is explained as follows. Owing to the formation of F1s during the synthesis of the rGOs precursor in PFTBA solution (see the F1s peak at 678.8 eV in Fig. S1), it could be deduced that rGOs comprised highly fluorinated graphene with numerous defects. The C–C bond in CF–CFn (where n = 2,3) was weak and the bonding could be easily broken, which led to the evaporation of CFn fragments [28,29]. When the temperature of the hydrothermal process was sufficiently high (>160 °C), rGOs could be cut into scattered rGOQDs and reduced to oxygen-containing groups after the evaporation of the F content. The CFn groups located near structure defects act as scissors and convert rGOs into rGOQDs during the pyrolysis process, suggesting that this proposed reduction mechanism of PFTBA to oxygen-containing groups is a facile way of producing rGOQDs using green solvent.

To characterize the optical properties of rGOQDs, absorption and PL spectra were measured (Fig. 4 ). The result of the absorption spectrum measured from 250 nm to 575 nm shows a strong peak in the range of 263 to 280 nm, which suggests the π–π* transition [30] with a gradual decreasing of the intensity. The PL spectra measured under UV irradiation revealed that the location of the emission peak shifted to a shorter wavelength, from 489.3 nm to 460.8 nm, as the solution temperature increased from 160 °C to 190 °C, respectively. Since the shift of the PL peak to the lower wavelength is due to the enhancement of the bandgap attributed to the reduced particle size [31], it is claimed that our study provides an efficient method of controlling the size of the rGOQDs. The results of the absorption spectrum and PL spectra are shown in Fig. 4(a). To study the compositional and structural properties of rGOQDs, excitation-wavelength dependent fluorescence (EWDF) data were measured. As shown in Fig. 4(b), the PL intensity in the EWDF map gradually decreased as the excitation-wavelength increased. In fact, a redshift of the fluorescent peak from 448 nm to 502 nm was observed as the excitation wavelengths changed from 290 nm to 380 nm. It is known that the fluorescence-wavelength-redshift is noted when the intermediate energy level between the highest occupied and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital is changed [32].

PL lifetime of the rGOQDs solution was measured by using a pulsed laser having a wavelength of 375 nm. The fitting results which follow a bi-exponentially decaying curve [33] yielded fast- and slow-decay time constants of 1.5 ns and 6.9 ns, respectively, and their corresponding relative amplitudes were 75 % and 25 % (Fig. 4(c)). The photostability of the rGOQDs sample was estimated by applying an external excitation. As shown in Fig. 4(d), the continuous irradiation of UV for 80 min produced a stable PL signal without decay in intensity. To evaluate the long-term durability, PL signals were measured for 30 days of exposure, but no decrease in intensity or change in the shape of the spectra was observed (Fig. S3). These results demonstrate that the rGOQDs synthesized by the proposed method will fulfill one of the most crucial requirements for applications in optical sensing.

For the possible application of the sample for the colorimetric detection of H2O2 and glucose, PL spectra from the rGOQD-solution mixed with glucose oxidase (GOx) were measured, and the change of color was analyzed following the standard detection mechanism. When glucose is added to glucose oxidase GOx, it generates H2O2 following the catalytic reaction of C6H12O6 + O2 (+ GOx) → C6H10O6 + H2O2, as illustrated in Fig. 5 (a). Depending on H2O2 concentration, the oxidation state of rGOQDs varied, and the color of PL changed from blue to green, accordingly. The change of the PL-peak position and intensity is explained by the modification in the intermediate energy level of rGOQDs and by the destruction of surface passivation, which causes the PL quenching [34,35].

To evaluate the performance of prepared rGOQDs for the glucose sensor, different concentrations of H2O2 from 0 mM to 1.5 mM were prepared, and the intensity and peak shift of PL under UV irradiation were measured. The wavelengths of the PL changed significantly, from 497.4 nm to 516.7 nm, as the concentrations of H2O2 were varied from 0 mM to 1.5 mM, respectively, which enabled us to analyze the chromaticity (see Fig. S4). The performance of colorimetric biosensors based on graphene quantum dots prepared via normal technique [36–38] and the current synthesis method is summarized in Table 1 .

In addition, the glucose concentration was estimated by analyzing the change of the PL spectra under the UV irradiation for GOx mixed rGOQDs solution by using a portable smartphone equipped with a UV long-pass filter. The measured color was digitized following the Hue algorithm. The details of the calculated chromaticity [r,g,b] and hue values provided in the Hue algorithm are described in the supplementary information. As the glucose concentration was increased from 0 mM to 2 mM, the chromaticity value of blue decreased from 0.6159 to 0.5703 while that of green increased from 0.3828 to 0.4273. Further, the hue value, which was defined as the single change in intrinsic color value, decreased from 202.8 to 195.1 in the same glucose concentration range. The sensitivity of glucose detection was derived by the fit using a linear regression equation and was determined to be −3.869 (degree/mM). The glucose concentration was finally derived from the hue values derived as follows [39],
c
=
( H - 202.526 )
- 3.869
The detection limit, defined by 3ΔH/3.869 where ΔH is the standard deviation of the hue value, was 0.371 mM.

The selectivity of the rGOQDs-based colorimetric sensor was evaluated by calculating the sensitivities for glucose, fructose, sucrose and various minerals (Zn2+, K+, Ca2+, and Fe+). The concentrations of all tested solutions were set to the same (2 mM) in GOx-containing solution. The sensitivity of the hue value, SH, defined as S H = (H a − H b )/H b, where H b and H a are hue values measured before and after adding the tested solution, respectively, yielded that glucose exhibited the highest SH of all analyzed solution. The relative sensitivities normalized to glucose are illustrated in Fig. 5(c). All these results demonstrated that rGOQDs in GOx-containing PFTBA solution are effectively applied for a glucose sensor using a portable smartphone.

In this study, we designed a facile one-pot synthesis method for the fabrication of rGOQDs using rGOs as precursors and evaluated the possible application for optical devices. The rGOQDs samples were immersed in the same solvent throughout the entire process. Without the use of toxic elements, the production of undesirable functional groups was effectively suppressed. Produced rGOQDs exhibited high photostability under constant irradiation of UV longer than 80 min without any decay in peak intensity. The analytic results of PL image measured using a portable smartphone showed that the rGOQDs has a high sensitivity (−3.869 degree/mM) which is suitable for applications on a colorimetric-based sensor. Our combined results on optical and structural studies provide an environmentally friendly method to synthesize rGOQDs, which can be used to measure glucose concentration.

Juil Hwang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Anh Duy Duong Le: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Cong Tai Trinh: Investigation, Methodology. Quynh Thuy Le: Investigation, Methodology. Kwang-Geol Lee: Resources, Supervision, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Jaeyong Kim: Resources, Supervision, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education, Science and Technology (NRF-2016K1A4A3914691, 2018R1D1AB07049811, and 2019M1A7A1A03088471), and Institute of Information & Communications Technology Planning & Evaluation (IITP) grant funded by the Korea government (MSIT) (No. 2019-0-00296).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.sna.2020.112495.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Energy gaps in zero-dimensional graphene nanoribbons

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Shemella, Y. Zhang, M. Mailman, P. M. Ajayan, S. K. Nayak, Energy gaps in zero-dimensional graphene nanoribbons, Appl. Phys. Lett. 91 (2007) 042101.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Luminescence properties of boron and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots prepared from arc-discharge-generated doped graphene samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Dey, A. Govindaraj, K. Biswas, C. N. R. Rao, Luminescence properties of boron and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots prepared from arc-discharge-generated doped graphene samples, Chem. Phys. Lett. 595 (2014) 203-208.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chaotic Dirac billiard in graphene quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. A. Ponomarenko, F. Schedin, M. I. Katsnelson, R. Yang, E. W. Hill, K. S. Novoselov, A. K. Geim, Chaotic Dirac billiard in graphene quantum dots, Science (Washington, DC, U. S.) 320 (2008) 356-358.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: InP/ZnS–graphene oxide and reduced graphene oxide nanocomposites as fascinating materials for potential optoelectronic applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Samal, P. Mohapatra, R. Subbiah, C. L. Lee, B. Anass, J. A. Kim, T. Kim and D. K. Yi, InP/ZnS–graphene oxide and reduced graphene oxide nanocomposites as fascinating materials for potential optoelectronic applications, Nanosale, 20 (2013) 9793-9805.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Performance and stability of counter electrodes based on reduced few-layer graphene oxide sheets and reduced graphene oxide quantum dots for dye-sensitized solar cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Torres, D. Sebastián, M. J. Lázaro, J. L. Pinilla, I. Suelves, A. S. Aricò, V. Baglio, Performance and stability of counter electrodes based on reduced few-layer graphene oxide sheets and reduced graphene oxide quantum dots for dye-sensitized solar cells, Electrochimica Acta, 306 (2019) 396-406.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Low-temperature titania-graphene quantum dots paste for flexible dye-sensitised solar cell applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. K. Kumar, D. Suazo-Davila, D. García-Torres, N. P. Cook, A. Ivaturi, M. H. Hsu, H. M. Upadhyaya, Low-temperature titania-graphene quantum dots paste for flexible dye-sensitised solar cell applications, Electrochimica Acta, 305 (2019) 278-284.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sulphur doping: a facile approach to tune the electronic structure and optical properties of graphene quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Peng, W. Gao, B. K. Gupta, Graphene quantum dots derived from carbon fibers. Nano Lett. 12 (2012), 844-829.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene quantum dots derived from carbon fibers

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Peng, W. Gao, B. K. Gupta, Graphene quantum dots derived from carbon fibers. Nano Lett. 12 (2012), 844-829.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of strongly fluorescent graphene quantum dots by cage-opening buckminsterfullerene

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. K. Chua, Z. Sofer, P. Šimek, O. Jankovský, K. Klímová, S. Bakardjieva, Synthesis of strongly fluorescent graphene quantum dots by cage-opening buckminsterfullerene. ACS Nano 9 (2015) 2548-2555.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation of graphene oxide by solvent-free mechanochemical oxidation of graphite

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Posudievsky, O. A. Khazieieva, V. G. Koshechko, V. D. Pokhodenko, Preparation of graphene oxide by solvent-free mechanochemical oxidation of graphite. J. Mater. Chem. A 22 (2012) 12465-12467.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene oxide quantum dots as the support for the synthesis of gold nanoparticles and their applications as new catalysts for the decomposition of composite solid propellants

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. P. Melo, P. L. Rios, P. Povea, C. M. Verdejo, M. B. Camarada, Graphene Oxide Quantum Dots as the Support for the Synthesis of Gold Nanoparticles and Their Applications as New Catalysts for the Decomposition of Composite Solid Propellants. ACS Omega 7 (2018) 7278-7287.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots as new hole transport material for perovskite solar cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Paulo, G. Stoica, W. Cambarau, E. Martinez-Ferrero, E. Palomares, Carbon quantum dots as new hole transport material for perovskite solar cells. Synth. Met. 222 (2016) 17-22.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrothermal route for cutting graphene sheets into blue‐luminescent graphene quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Y. Pan, J. C. Zhang, Z. Li, M. H. Wu, Hydrothermal route for cutting graphene sheets into blue‐luminescent graphene quantum dots. Adv. Mater. 22 (2010) 734-738.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photo-Fenton reaction of graphene oxide: a new strategy to prepare graphene quantum dots for DNA cleavage

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Zhou, Y. Zhang, C. Wang, X. Wu, Y. Yang, B. Zheng, H. Wu, S. Guo, J. Zhang, Photo-Fenton reaction of graphene oxide: a new strategy to prepare graphene quantum dots for DNA cleavage, ACS Nano 6 (2012) 6592-6599.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Novel synthesis of slightly fluorinated graphene quantum dots with luminescent and paramagnetic properties through thermal cutting of fluorinated graphene

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Feng, Q. Xiao, Y. Liu, Y. Zheng, Y. Lin, J. Li, Q. Ye, Z. Huang, Novel synthesis of slightly fluorinated graphene quantum dots with luminescent and paramagnetic properties through thermal cutting of fluorinated graphene. Materials 11 (2018) 91.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effective reduction of graphene oxide thin films by a fluorinating agent: diethylaminosulfur trifluoride

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Gao, X. Tang, Effective reduction of graphene oxide thin films by a fluorinating agent: diethylaminosulfur trifluoride. Carbon 76 (2014) 133-140.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Can fluorine chemistry be green chemistry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. J. Tavener, J. H. Clark, Can fluorine chemistry be green chemistry, J. Fluorine Chem. 123 (2003) 31-36.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon‐based dots co‐doped with nitrogen and sulfur for high quantum yield and excitation‐independent emission

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Dong, H. Pang, H. B. Yang, C. Guo, J. Shao, Y. Chi, C. M. Li, T. Yu., Carbon‐based dots co‐doped with nitrogen and sulfur for high quantum yield and excitation‐independent emission, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 52 (2013) 7800-7804.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fabrication of graphene quantum dot-decorated graphene sheets via chemical surface modification

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Ryu, E. Lee, S. Lee, J. Jang, Fabrication of graphene quantum dot-decorated graphene sheets via chemical surface modification, Chem. Commun. 50 (2014) 15616-15618.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoluminescence and Raman studies of graphene thin films prepared by reduction of graphene oxide

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. V. Cuong, V. H. Pham, Q. T. Tran, S. H. Hahn, J. S. Chung, E. W. Shin, E. J. Kim, Photoluminescence and Raman studies of graphene thin films prepared by reduction of graphene oxide, Materials letters, 64(3) (2010) 399-401.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A comparison between Raman spectroscopy and surface characterizations of multiwall carbon nanotubes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Delhaes, M. Couzi, M. Trinquecoste, J. Dentzer, H. Hamidou, C. Vix‐Guterl, A comparison between Raman spectroscopy and surface characterizations of multiwall carbon nanotubes, Carbon 44 (2006) 3005-3013.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Colorimetric multienzymatic smart sensors for hydrogen peroxide, glucose and catechol screening analysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: O. Hosu, M. Lettieri, N. Papara, A. Ravalli, R. Sandulescu, C. Cristea, G. Marrazza, Colorimetric multienzymatic smart sensors for hydrogen peroxide, glucose and catechol screening analysis, Talanta 204 (2019) 525.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jaeyong Kim: received his Ph. D. degree in Physic at Washington University, St. Louis, USA in 1999. Currently he is a full professor at Physics Department of Hanyang University, in Seoul, Korea, with research emphasis on synthesis and characterization of emergent properties from quantum materials.

Kwang-Geol Lee: is an associate professor of Physics at Hanyang University in Korea (2012-current). He has received his Ph.D. in 2007 from Seoul National University, Korea. During his postdoctoral period, he continued to study the light-matter interactions at nano-scales playing with optical antennas at ETH Zurich, Switzerland (2008–2011) and at Max-Planck Institute in Erlangen, Germany (2011). His current research interests are the generation of quantum light sources based on quantum emitters, and quantum optical sensing/imaging.

Juil Hwang: is presently a Ph.D. candidate in Physics from Hanyang University, South Korea. His main research areas include photonic structures for nano emitters and algorithm design for optical sensing/imaging system.

Anh Duy Duong Le: received his B.S. degree in Ho Chi Minh City University of Science (HCMUS), Vietnam National University, Vietnam in 2014. He has received the Ph. D. degree in Physics at Hanyang University, Korea in 2020. His research interests are developing optical sensors of photonic devices, nonlinear optics and quantum dot materials.

Cong Tai Trinh: received the Ph.D. degree in Physics from Hanyang University, South Korea in 2020. Currently, as a postdoctoral researcher, his research interests are the photophysics of single quantum emitter, and their applications in quantum light sources, and optoelectronic devices.

Quynh Thuy Le: graduated (BSc degree in Chemistry) at Hanoi University of Science, VNU, Viet Nam in 2014 and obtained her Master’s degree in Organic chemistry at Hanoi University of Science, Viet Nam in 2017. Currently, she is a Ph.D candidate at Chemistry Department of Hanyang University, Seoul, Korea with a research topic of total synthesis of icetexane diterpenoid.
